class_schedule.topic: Construction Project Lifecycle and Delivery Methods
learning_objectives: Describe the construction project lifecycle from feasibility to closeout
Compare delivery methods: Design-Bid-Build, Design-Build, CM at Risk, IPD
Understand the roles of owner, architect, general contractor, and subcontractors

lecture_notes.introduction: Construction is one of the oldest project-based industries. Its methods and challenges provide foundational lessons for all project managers.
key_concepts: Lifecycle: feasibility, design, procurement, construction, commissioning, closeout
Design-Bid-Build: sequential, owner holds separate design and construction contracts
Design-Build: single entity responsible for both design and construction
CM at Risk: construction manager provides guaranteed maximum price during design
Integrated Project Delivery (IPD): shared risk/reward among owner, designer, builder
lecture_notes.demonstration: Comparing delivery methods for a $20M university science building project

review.value: Most traditional; clear separation of design and construction but no contractor input during design; change orders are common
Shared risk/reward contract aligns incentives; requires high trust and collaboration; reduces adversarial relationships

learning_objectives: Build a construction schedule using CPM with resource loading
Apply the Last Planner System for construction schedule reliability
Manage weather, permitting, and inspection scheduling constraints
readings: Ballard, The Last Planner System of Production Control

lecture_notes.introduction: Construction schedules are unique because of physical dependencies, weather constraints, and the coordination of dozens of specialty trades.
key_concepts: Construction CPM: physical dependencies (foundation before framing), resource dependencies, external dependencies
Resource loading: assigning labor crews, equipment, and materials to activities
Last Planner System: plan, look-ahead, weekly work plan, percent plan complete (PPC)
Weather days: allowances and tracking for weather-related delays
Inspection milestones: building inspections required before proceeding to next phase
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a CPM schedule for a single-story commercial building with resource loading and weather allowances
setup_requirements: Construction activity list with typical durations

review.value: Plans made by the people doing the work (foremen); PPC measures plan reliability and drives continuous improvement
Cannot install plumbing before framing; cannot frame before foundation cures; these are mandatory dependencies

learning_objectives: Perform quantity takeoff from construction drawings
Build a construction cost estimate with labor, material, equipment, and overhead
readings: RS Means Quick Cost Estimating Guide

lecture_notes.introduction: Construction cost estimating starts with counting physical quantities from drawings and pricing each item with current rates.
key_concepts: Quantity takeoff: measuring areas, volumes, lengths, and counts from construction drawings
Unit cost estimating: cost per cubic yard of concrete, per linear foot of piping, per square foot of flooring
Cost categories: direct costs (labor, material, equipment), indirect costs (supervision, temporary facilities), markup (overhead + profit)
Contingency: typically 5-15% depending on design completeness
Bid preparation: bid bond, bid opening, low-bid selection, protest procedures
lecture_notes.demonstration: Performing a quantity takeoff and cost estimate for a concrete foundation from provided drawings

review.value: The most time-consuming but critical step; errors in quantity directly translate to cost estimate errors
General contractor markup covers home office overhead, insurance, bonding, and profit; typically 10-20% of direct costs

learning_objectives: Implement a construction safety program compliant with OSHA regulations
Apply construction quality management including inspections and testing
Manage construction documentation: submittals, RFIs, and change orders
readings: OSHA Construction Safety Standards (29 CFR 1926) overview

lecture_notes.introduction: Construction is one of the most dangerous industries. Safety is not optional, it is the project manager's first responsibility.
key_concepts: OSHA requirements: fall protection, scaffolding, excavation, electrical, PPE
Safety program elements: training, hazard assessment, incident reporting, safety meetings
Quality management: material testing, inspection holds, non-conformance reports
Submittals: shop drawings, material data, and samples for architect approval before procurement
RFIs (Request for Information): formal process for clarifying design ambiguities
lecture_notes.demonstration: Reviewing a construction safety plan and conducting a mock job site safety inspection
setup_requirements: Sample submittal log and RFI log

review.value: Violations result in fines ($15K+ per serious violation), project shutdowns, and potential criminal charges for willful violations
Formal written question from contractor to designer; response becomes part of the contract documents; delays in RFI responses delay construction

class_schedule.topic: Construction Claims, Disputes, and Project Closeout
learning_objectives: Identify and document construction claims for time and cost
Navigate dispute resolution in construction contracts
Execute construction project closeout including punch lists and warranties
readings: AIA A201 General Conditions (claims and disputes sections)

lecture_notes.introduction: Construction projects frequently involve claims and disputes. Proper documentation and process management protect all parties.
key_concepts: Claim types: differing site conditions, design errors, owner-caused delays, change order disputes
Documentation: daily logs, photographs, contemporaneous records, correspondence
AIA dispute resolution: initial decision by architect, mediation, arbitration
Punch list: list of incomplete or defective items to be corrected before final payment
Substantial completion vs. final completion: occupancy allowed vs. all work 100% done
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing a construction delay claim with supporting schedule analysis and documentation

review.value: The point where the owner can use the facility for its intended purpose; triggers warranty period and final payment retention release
The single most important document for claim support; record weather, manpower, equipment, work performed, and delays daily

learning_objectives: Evaluate construction technology: BIM, drones, prefabrication
Apply green building and sustainability principles to construction projects
Understand LEED certification requirements and process

lecture_notes.introduction: Construction technology is transforming the industry. Sustainability is no longer optional for many projects.
key_concepts: BIM (Building Information Modeling): 3D model with data for design coordination and clash detection
Drones: site surveys, progress monitoring, safety inspections
Prefabrication and modular construction: factory-built components assembled on site
LEED certification: sustainable sites, water efficiency, energy, materials, indoor quality
Embodied carbon: the carbon footprint of construction materials and processes
lecture_notes.demonstration: Reviewing a BIM model and identifying design clashes between structural, mechanical, and electrical systems

review.value: Enables clash detection before construction, reducing costly field rework; also supports facility management after construction
Points-based system; Certified, Silver, Gold, Platinum levels based on total points across sustainability categories

questions.question: An owner wants to build a hospital. The design is complex, the budget is tight, and the owner wants contractor input during design. Which delivery method should be used and why?
questions.answer: CM at Risk (Construction Manager at Risk) is the best choice because: (1) The CM provides preconstruction services during design, giving the owner contractor expertise on cost, constructibility, and scheduling while design develops, (2) The GMP (Guaranteed Maximum Price) provides budget certainty for the tight budget, (3) The CM can identify cost-saving alternatives during design before it is too late to change, (4) Hospital construction is complex with stringent MEP coordination that benefits from early contractor involvement. Design-Build would also provide early contractor input but gives the owner less control over design. IPD would require cultural readiness and is less common for healthcare.

rubric.key: Selects CM at Risk with clear rationale
Explains contractor input during design benefit

questions.question: Explain how the Last Planner System differs from traditional CPM scheduling and why it improves schedule reliability in construction.
questions.answer: Traditional CPM creates a top-down schedule by the project manager or scheduler, often months in advance, that pushes work to the field. The Last Planner System creates a bottom-up plan where foremen (the 'last planners' who assign work directly to crews) make commitments only for work they are confident they CAN do in the next week. It improves reliability because: (1) Commitments are made by the people closest to the work who understand actual constraints, (2) Look-ahead planning (6 weeks) identifies and removes constraints before work is scheduled, (3) Percent Plan Complete (PPC) is tracked weekly, creating accountability and data for improvement, (4) Reasons for plan failures are categorized to identify systemic issues. Studies show PPC improvements from 50% to 80%+ correlate with project performance improvement.

rubric.key: Correctly contrasts top-down CPM with bottom-up LPS
Explains the role of the 'last planner'
Identifies PPC and constraint removal as key mechanisms

questions.question: A foundation requires 150 cubic yards of concrete at $180/CY placed, 12,000 pounds of rebar at $1.20/lb installed, and 4,800 square feet of formwork at $8.50/SF. Calculate the direct cost and add a 10% contingency.

rubric.key: Correct calculation for each line item

questions.question: A worker falls from a scaffold that lacked guardrails. What OSHA requirements were violated, what are the immediate actions the project manager must take, and what are the potential consequences?
questions.answer: OSHA violations: 29 CFR 1926.451 requires guardrails on all open sides of scaffolds above 10 feet; mid-rails, top-rails, and toe-boards are required. Immediate actions: (1) Render first aid and call emergency services, (2) Secure the accident scene and prevent further exposure, (3) Notify OSHA within 8 hours (24 hours for hospitalization), (4) Document the incident with photographs, witness statements, and written report, (5) Conduct an incident investigation to determine root cause, (6) Implement corrective actions (install guardrails on all scaffolds) before resuming work. Consequences: OSHA serious violation fine ($15,625+ per violation), potential willful violation fine ($156,259) if guardrails were knowingly omitted, project shutdown until compliance, workers compensation claim, potential criminal charges if willful, and reputational damage.

major_assignments.title: Construction Project Management Plan
major_assignments.description: Develop a construction project management plan for a commercial building project including delivery method selection, schedule, cost estimate, safety plan, and quality management plan.
objectives: Select and justify a project delivery method
Build a CPM schedule with resource loading for the construction phase
Prepare a cost estimate with quantity takeoff for major work items
Develop a site-specific safety plan and quality management approach
deliverables: Delivery method analysis and selection (2-3 pages)
CPM schedule in Microsoft Project or Primavera with resource loading
Cost estimate spreadsheet with quantity takeoff for major items
Quality management plan with inspection and testing protocols (2-3 pages)

criteria: Selection justified based on project characteristics
CPM logic is correct with realistic durations
Resource loading shows labor and equipment assignments
Critical path identified and float analyzed
Quantity takeoff covers major work items
Unit costs are realistic and sourced
Quality inspections and testing requirements defined
Accurate cost estimate with proper methodology

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on delivery methods, scheduling, estimating, and safety

midterm_examination.description: Delivery methods, scheduling, and cost estimating

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all construction PM topics

class_participation.description: Participation in scheduling exercises, cost estimating workshops, and safety reviews

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Estimating and scheduling workshops require in-person participation.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 5 days past the due date.
course_policies.collaboration: Estimating exercises may be done in pairs. The construction PM plan is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Cost data must be sourced from published references (RS Means or equivalent).
course_policies.technology_requirements: Microsoft Project or Primavera P6 (trial). Spreadsheet application for cost estimating. BIM viewer for technology session.
